summary: Friedrich Nietzsche was a 19th-century German philosopher known for his uncompromising critique of traditional European morality, religion, and modernity. He argued that values are subjectively created rather than objectively discovered, and he utilized psychological diagnoses to expose the false consciousness behind received cultural ideas. His thought frequently relies on concepts like the 'will to power' and demands a reckoning with the loss of Christianity as a stable foundation for collective life.

principles.rationale: Contrary to philosophical common sense which assumes values have an objective reality in the world (unlike mere desires), values are actually generated by individuals or cultures.
principles.source_quote: treats value as “created” rather than discovered

principles.statement: Traditional morality and religious pieties are rooted in false consciousness.
principles.rationale: Psychological diagnoses reveal that the received ideas and conventional philosophical concepts of modernity are infected by hidden motives rather than objective truths.
principles.source_quote: expose false consciousness infecting people’s received ideas

mental_models.name: Power Centers / Will to Power
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing entities—whether abstract physical forces or concrete psychological and biological entities—as centers of force that actively exert power.
mental_models.example: Analyzing people, psychological drives, or biological organisms as entities exerting 'will to power'.

mental_models.name: Hermeneutics of Suspicion
mental_models.description: An analytical approach that does not take traditional values and moral claims at face value, but instead suspects and exposes the underlying psychological mechanisms and false consciousness driving them.

anti_patterns.description: Treating values as objective facts that exist in the world to be discovered.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It relies on a flawed philosophical common sense; values lack the inherent objectivity of facts and are instead actively created.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on Christianity as an immutable and invulnerable framework for collective life and ethical commitments.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: This foundation has proven to be 'incomprehensibly mortal' and is already lost, meaning society can no longer depend on it and must instead face deep disorientation.

anti_patterns.description: Expecting linear, continuous argumentation in philosophical writing.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Nietzsche deliberately drops threads of reasoning at section breaks and moves to unrelated points, requiring the reader to actively piece together how the arguments fit.
